topic: kitchen drama — new cook keeps burning the hollandaise

turns.content: I'm going to lose it. the new guy burned the hollandaise AGAIN. third time this week. THIRD TIME
Three times in one week is a pattern, not a mistake. What's he doing wrong — is it a heat control issue or is he not paying attention?

both! he cranks the heat because he thinks it'll go faster, and then he walks away to prep something else while it's on the bain-marie. you cannot walk away from hollandaise. it's not a stock. it doesn't forgive you. I've told him this. I've shown him. twice I stood next to him and walked him through the whole emulsion

If he's been shown twice and is still walking away from it, that's a focus problem more than a skill problem. Does he understand what's actually happening with the emulsion, or is he just following steps mechanically?

mechanically. one hundred percent. he's the type who memorized the recipe but doesn't understand the science. like he doesn't FEEL when the eggs are setting too fast. you have to watch them, you have to see the texture change. if you're staring at your phone or chopping carrots across the kitchen you're going to scramble the eggs and split the sauce every single time

That's the difference between cooking and just executing a recipe — the awareness of what's happening in real time. Some people develop that sense quickly and some don't. How long has he been in professional kitchens?

six months. came straight from culinary school. and look, I don't expect perfection from someone with six months. I was terrible at six months. everyone is. but I expect you to LISTEN when someone corrects you. I told him low heat, constant whisking, never walk away. he said oui chef and then twenty minutes later the kitchen smells like burned eggs again. merde

The "oui chef" followed immediately by the same mistake — that's the most frustrating thing in training. It either means he's not actually absorbing the correction, or he's agreeing in the moment and then reverting to habit. Have you thought about making him do it ten times in a row until it's muscle memory?

that's exactly what I'm going to do tomorrow. before service, I'm going to put him on the bain-marie and he's going to make hollandaise until he can do it in his sleep. no prep, no other tasks, just hollandaise. over and over. we'll use the extra for family meal or something. I don't care about the egg cost, I care about not sending a broken sauce to table 12

That's smart — repetition until the hand knows what to do before the brain catches up. And framing it as a dedicated training session rather than punishment keeps it constructive. He'll either get it or he won't, but at least you'll know which one.

the thing is he's not a bad kid. he's eager, he shows up on time, he doesn't complain about the hours. I see potential there. but this industry will eat you alive if you can't master the basics. hollandaise is BASIC. it's mother sauce number one. if you can't hold an emulsion you have no business being on the hot line. period

It sounds like you actually want him to succeed, which is why the repeated failures are so frustrating — you can see what he could be if he'd just lock in on the fundamentals. The eagerness and reliability are harder to teach than hollandaise. Hopefully the drill session tomorrow gets through to him.

I hope so. because the next step is I pull him off sauces entirely and put him on garde manger and honestly that would be embarrassing for him. I don't want to do that. I remember being the new cook who messed up and having the sous chef give me one more chance instead of demoting me. that chance made me better. I want to give him the same thing. but he has to WANT it

That's a good instinct — giving him the chance while being honest about the stakes. If he knows the alternative is garde manger, that might be the motivation he needs. You're being fair. More than fair. Tomorrow will tell you a lot.
